title: Cat Unable to Blink One Eye: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Find out why your cat cannot blink one eye, if it is a veterinary emergency, and how a photo can help your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
What is it when a cat cannot blink?

When a cat is unable to blink one eye, it is often a sign of facial nerve paralysis or an issue with the palpebral reflex. The facial nerve controls the muscles used for blinking and facial expressions. If this nerve is damaged due to inflammation, ear infections, trauma, or idiopathic reasons, the eyelid may remain open, leaving the eye vulnerable to drying out.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency level: Low. While seeing your cat unable to blink can be distressing, it is generally not a life-threatening emergency if the cat is otherwise acting normally. However, because the eye cannot properly lubricate itself without blinking, there is a risk of developing corneal ulcers or secondary infections. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent long-term damage to the eye surface.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's face can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian. A photo allows the vet to assess facial symmetry, look for drooping of the lip or ear on the affected side, and check for signs of ocular discharge or redness. This visual information helps the triage team determine if the issue is localized to the eye or part of a larger neurological concern.

faqs.question: Could an ear infection cause my cat to stop blinking?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the facial nerve passes through the middle ear, a deep ear infection can cause inflammation that leads to temporary facial paralysis.

faqs.question: How can I protect my cat's eye until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: You can consult your vet about using a plain, preservative-free artificial tear lubricant to keep the cornea moist, but avoid any medicated drops without professional advice.

faqs.question: Will my cat's ability to blink return?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the underlying cause. If it is due to inflammation or infection, blinking often returns once the primary issue is treated. In some cases, it may take several weeks or may be permanent.
